### Ropeways and cable cars

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kathmandu&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: Ropeways and cable cars")]

[Ropeways in Nepal](/wiki/Ropeways_in_Nepal "Ropeways in Nepal") are an important transportation means in hilly terrain.[[105]](#cite_note-107) A ropeway operated between Kathmandu and [Hetauda](/wiki/Hetauda "Hetauda") over a length of 43 km (27 mi) which carried 25 tonnes of goods per hour. Service was discontinued due to poor carrying capacity and maintenance issues. During the Rana period, a ropeway was constructed between [Matatirtha](/wiki/Matatirtha "Matatirtha") in Kathmandu to Dhorsing in [Makwanpur District](/wiki/Makwanpur_District "Makwanpur District") of over 22 km (14 mi) in length, which carried a cargo of 8 tonnes per hour. At present, cable cars are operated from Kathmandu to the [Chandragiri Hills](/wiki/Chandragiri_Cable_Car "Chandragiri Cable Car").[[106]](#cite_note-108)

## Media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kathmandu&action=edit&section=48 "Edit section: Media")]

Kathmandu is the television hub of Nepal. [Nepal Television](/wiki/Nepal_Television "Nepal Television") (established in 1984) is the oldest and most-watched television channel in Nepal, as is government-owned [NTV PLUS](/wiki/NTV_PLUS "NTV PLUS") and also [Kantipur Television](/wiki/Kantipur_Television "Kantipur Television"), [Image Channel](/wiki/Image_Channel "Image Channel"), [Sagarmatha Television](/wiki/Sagarmatha_Television_(Nepal) "Sagarmatha Television (Nepal)"), [Himalaya TV](/wiki/Himalaya_TV "Himalaya TV"), [AP1 TV](/wiki/AP1_TV "AP1 TV"), and other channels.

The headquarters of many of the country's news outlets are also in the city including Kathmandu Tribune, the government-owned [Gorkhapatra](/wiki/Gorkhapatra "Gorkhapatra") (the oldest national daily newspaper in Nepal), [The Kathmandu Post](/wiki/The_Kathmandu_Post "The Kathmandu Post"), [Nepali Times](/wiki/Nepali_Times "Nepali Times"), [Kantipur Publications](/wiki/Kantipur_Publications "Kantipur Publications") and its paper [Kantipur](/wiki/Kantipur_(daily) "Kantipur (daily)"), [Naya Patrika](/wiki/Naya_Patrika "Naya Patrika"), [The Himalayan Times](/wiki/The_Himalayan_Times "The Himalayan Times"), [Karobar Economic Daily](/wiki/Karobar_Economic_Daily "Karobar Economic Daily"), Aarthik Abhiyan National Daily and [Jana Aastha National Weekly](/wiki/Jana_Aastha_National_Weekly "Jana Aastha National Weekly").

Nepal Republic Media, the publisher of [myRepublica](/wiki/Republica_(newspaper) "Republica (newspaper)"), joined a publishing alliance with the [International Herald Tribune](/wiki/International_Herald_Tribune "International Herald Tribune") (IHT) to publish the Asia Pacific Edition of IHT from Kathmandu from 20 July 2011. There is also a state-run [National News Agency (RSS)](/wiki/Rastriya_Samachar_Samiti "Rastriya Samachar Samiti").

[Radio Nepal](/wiki/Radio_Nepal "Radio Nepal") is a state-run organization that operates national and regional radio stations; these stations are: [Hits FM](/wiki/Hits_FM_(Nepal) "Hits FM (Nepal)"), [Radio Kantipur](/wiki/Radio_Kantipur "Radio Kantipur"), HBC 94 FM, Radio Sagarmatha and Image FM. The [BBC](/wiki/BBC_Radio "BBC Radio") also has an FM broadcasting station in Kathmandu. Some [community radio](/wiki/Community_radio "Community radio") stations such as Radio Pratibodh and Radio Upatyaka also broadcast within the valley.

## Sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kathmandu&action=edit&section=49 "Edit section: Sports")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f6/Dasarath_Stadium_during_2022_SAFF_Women%27s_Championship_Final_match.jpg/250px-Dasarath_Stadium_during_2022_SAFF_Women%27s_Championship_Final_match.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dasarath_Stadium_during_2022_SAFF_Women%27s_Championship_Final_match.jpg)

[Dasharath Rangasala](/wiki/Dasharath_Rangasala "Dasharath Rangasala")

[Cricket](/wiki/Cricket "Cricket"), [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") and [volleyball](/wiki/Volleyball "Volleyball") are the most popular sports among the younger generation in Nepal and there are several stadiums in the city.[[107]](#cite_note-109) The sport is governed by the [National Sports Council](/wiki/National_Sports_Council_(Nepal) "National Sports Council (Nepal)") from its headquarters in Kathmandu. The only international football stadium in the city is the [Dasharath Rangasala](/wiki/Dasharath_Rangasala "Dasharath Rangasala"), a [multi-purpose stadium](/wiki/Multi-purpose_stadium "Multi-purpose stadium") used mostly for football matches and cultural events, in the neighbourhood of Tripureshwor. Built in 1956, it is the largest stadium in Nepal with a capacity of 25,000 spectators. The [Martyr's Memorial League](/wiki/Martyr%27s_Memorial_A-Division_League "Martyr's Memorial A-Division League") is also held in this ground every year. The stadium was renovated with Chinese aid before the [8th South Asian Games](/wiki/1999_South_Asian_Games "1999 South Asian Games") were held in Kathmandu in 1999 and [floodlights](/wiki/Floodlight "Floodlight") were installed. Kathmandu is home to the oldest football clubs of Nepal such as [Ranipokhari Corner Team](/wiki/Ranipokhari_Corner_Team "Ranipokhari Corner Team") (RCT), [Sankata Club](/wiki/Sankata_Boys_S.C. "Sankata Boys S.C.") and [New Road Team](/wiki/New_Road_Team "New Road Team") (NRT). Other prominent clubs include [Manang Marsyangdi Club](/wiki/Manang_Marshyangdi_Club "Manang Marshyangdi Club"), [Machhindra FC](/wiki/Machhindra_F.C. "Machhindra F.C."), [Tribhuvan Army Club](/wiki/Nepal_Army_Club "Nepal Army Club") (TAC) and [Nepal Police Club](/wiki/Nepal_Police_Club "Nepal Police Club").

Kathmandu is also home of some of the oldest cricket clubs in Nepal, such as Yengal Sports Club. [Kathmandu Gorkhas](/wiki/Kathmandu_Gorkhas "Kathmandu Gorkhas") represents Kathmandu in the [Nepal Premier League](/wiki/Nepal_Premier_League "Nepal Premier League").[[108]](#cite_note-110)

## International relations and organizations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kathmandu&action=edit&section=50 "Edit section: International relations and organizations")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/SAARC_Secretariat_at_Kathmandu.JPG/250px-SAARC_Secretariat_at_Kathmandu.JPG)](/wiki/File:SAARC_Secretariat_at_Kathmandu.JPG)

[South Asian Association for Regional Cooperation](/wiki/South_Asian_Association_for_Regional_Cooperation "South Asian Association for Regional Cooperation") Secretariat in Kathmandu

Kathmandu established an International Relations Secretariat in order to promote international relations. The city's first international relationship was established in 1975 with the city of [Eugene](/wiki/Eugene,_Oregon "Eugene, Oregon"), Oregon, United States; this was further enhanced by establishing formal relationships with 15 other cities:[[109]](#cite_note-111) [Matsumoto](/wiki/Matsumoto,_Nagano "Matsumoto, Nagano") (Japan), [Rochester](/wiki/Rochester,_New_York "Rochester, New York") (United States), [Fredericksburg](/wiki/Fredericksburg,_Virginia "Fredericksburg, Virginia") (United States), [Boulder](/wiki/Boulder,_Colorado "Boulder, Colorado") (United States), [Yangon](/wiki/Yangon "Yangon") (formerly Rangoon, Myanmar), [Xi'an](/wiki/Xi%27an "Xi'an") (China), [Shenzhen](/wiki/Shenzhen "Shenzhen") (China), [Chengdu](/wiki/Chengdu "Chengdu") (China), [Lhasa](/wiki/Lhasa_(city) "Lhasa (city)") (China), [Nanjing](/wiki/Nanjing "Nanjing") (China), [Lanzhou](/wiki/Lanzhou "Lanzhou") (China), [Minsk](/wiki/Minsk "Minsk") (Belarus), [Varanasi](/wiki/Varanasi "Varanasi") (India), [Seoul](/wiki/Seoul "Seoul") (South Korea) and [Pyongyang](/wiki/Pyongyang "Pyongyang") (North Korea).

Kathmandu's constant endeavour is to enhance its interaction with other South Asian countries, other international agencies and many other major cities of the world to achieve better urban management and developmental programs for Kathmandu.[[110]](#cite_note-relations-112) Kathmandu is home to several international and regional organizations, including the [South Asian Association for Regional Cooperation](/wiki/South_Asian_Association_for_Regional_Cooperation "South Asian Association for Regional Cooperation") (SAARC) and the [International Centre for Integrated Mountain Development](/wiki/International_Centre_for_Integrated_Mountain_Development "International Centre for Integrated Mountain Development") (ICIMOD).

The city is named after the [Kasthamandap](/wiki/Kasthamandap "Kasthamandap") temple, a 16th-century structure built entirely of wood from a single tree, located in the historic [Durbar Square](/wiki/Durbar_Square "Durbar Square"), which itself derives from the Sanskrit words *kāṣṭha* ("wood") and *maṇḍapa* ("pavilion").
